calendar.matters:
Order To Show Cause Re: Peremptory Writ Of Mandate
Off Calendar Apr-05-2018 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES: Attys. JOEL AURORA, ESQ. and CHRIS SKINNELL of Nielsen Merksamer Parrinello Gross & Leoni LLP (Tel: 415-389-6800) appearing for Petitioner Ismail Karajah;
Attys. EMILY A. ANDREWS, ESQ. and LANCE OLSON, ESQ. of Olson Hagel & Fishburn LLP (Tel: 916-442-2952) appearing for Real Party in Interest Malia Cohen;
Deputy City Attorney JOSHUA S. WHITE of the Office of the City Attorney - City and County of San Francisco (Tel: 415-554-4661) appearing for Respondent John Arntz, Director of the San Francisco Department of Elections, and Real Party in Interest San Francisco Ballot Simplification Committee.

The matter is argued and submitted by counsel for the parties.

The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

RULING: Petitioner Ismail Karajah petition for peremptory writ of mandate is granted as to the Argument in Favor of Proposition E and denied as to the Yes/No statements in the Official Digest for Proposition E. The parties stipulated to delete "the R.J. Reynolds Tobacco Company paid over $3 million to collect signatures to overturn the ordinance" and replace it with "The R.J. Reynolds Tobacco Company paid over $3 million to fund a campaign to overturn the ordinance" in the Argument in Favor of Proposition E and the court is agreeable to adopting the stipulation as an order on this writ. The language in the Yes/No statements in the Official Digest for Proposition E is not false, misleading or partial. While reasonable minds may differ whether it would have been preferred if the language in the Yes/No for Proposition E conformed to the sentence structure that has been used for other propositions that effectuate ordinances, the court's task is not to edit the summary language to perfection, but only to amend it upon clear and convincing evidence that it is false or misleading or partial. (Martinez v. Superior Court (2006) 142 Cal.App.4th 1245, 1248 ("To comply with the election statutes, the ballot title need not be the 'most accurate,' 'most comprehensive,' or 'fairest' that a skilled wordsmith might imagine. The title need only contain words that are neither false, misleading, nor partial. The title adopted by the city council meets that standard, and the judiciary is not free to substitute its judgment given its deferential standard of review.")). Unlike Huntington Beach City Council v. Superior Court (2002) 94 Cal.App.4th 1417 where the word "exemption" had a negative connotation or McDonough v. Superior Court (2012) 204 Cal.App.4th 1169 where the word "reform" had a positive connotation, the language in the Yes/No Statements for Proposition E is neutral and does not sway a voter one way or the other.

The Court authorizes a combined Order and Judgment which it signs in open court.

Judge: Harold Kahn; Clerk: Sean Kane; Reporter: Reyes F. Hunter, CSR #6576, email: rhunter@sftc.org. (302/HK)
